questions.question: Write the CUDA kernel for the basic trapezoidal rule using atomicAdd. Explain how each thread computes its contribution and how the boundary terms f(a) and f(b) are handled.
keyPoints: Kernel uses __global__ qualifier and takes parameters a, b, n, h, and a pointer to the result
Global thread index: my_i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
Each thread computes x_i = a + my_i * h and evaluates f(x_i)
Guard condition if (my_i <= n) prevents out-of-range threads from contributing
Boundary handling: if (my_i == 0 || my_i == n) multiply by 0.5 for the half-weight endpoints
atomicAdd(result_p, my_f) accumulates each thread's value into a single global variable

questions.question: Explain why the atomicAdd-based CUDA trapezoidal rule suffers from poor performance for large n. Describe the serialization bottleneck, the floating-point non-determinism issue, and how the improved version (using shared-memory reduction) addresses these limitations. Compare this approach with the analogous OpenMP critical section approach.
keyPoints: atomicAdd serializes concurrent writes: only one thread at a time can update the global result variable
With n threads all targeting the same address, the accumulation becomes O(n) instead of O(n/p)
The compute phase (evaluating f(x_i)) is fully parallel, but the accumulation phase destroys the speedup
Floating-point non-determinism: thread execution order varies between runs, and since FP addition is not associative, results differ in least significant bits
The improved version uses shared-memory parallel reduction within blocks (tree-based, O(log blockDim) steps), then combines block sums on the host
This is analogous to OpenMP: atomicAdd is like #pragma omp critical (serialized), while shared-memory reduction is like the reduction clause (efficient tree-based)
The shared-memory approach also provides more reproducible results since the reduction tree structure is fixed
